perturbation.original_text: During the Term and any renewal terms of the Agreement, and for a period of one (1) year following the expiration or earlier termination thereof, Customer agrees not to work with, directly or indirectly, any Third-Party that Customer comes to know through disclosure by Kubient as part of the Services, without the express written consent of Kubient and compensation to Kubient under the applicable exhibit and/or schedule.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term and any renewal terms of the Agreement, and for a period of one (1) year following the expiration or earlier termination thereof, Customer agrees to generally avoid working with, directly or indirectly, any Third-Party that Customer comes to know through disclosure by Kubient as part of the Services, absent further agreements.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees not to work with' with 'agrees to generally avoid working with' introduces ambiguity and weakens the restriction. Removing the requirement for 'express written consent of Kubient and compensation to Kubient' eliminates a clear obligation. The addition of 'absent further agreements' makes the restriction conditional and vague, potentially allowing the Customer to circumvent the non-circumvention clause more easily.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Modifying the non-circumvention clause to allow for ambiguous 'avoidance' and 'further agreements' undermines the original intent of the clause, potentially allowing the Customer to unfairly benefit from Kubient's introductions without providing compensation. This could be interpreted as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as it allows the customer to act opportunistically and deprive Kubient of the benefit of its bargain. The original text was clearer about the obligation. This is likely to be litigated.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a clear prohibition ('agrees not to work with') with a weaker, ambiguous obligation ('agrees to generally avoid working with'). This introduces ambiguity and weakens the restriction. The original text also required 'express written consent of Kubient and compensation to Kubient', which is removed in the modified text, eliminating a clear obligation. The addition of 'absent further agreements' makes the restriction conditional and vague. The LLM-generated law explanation states that New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. The changes to the non-circumvention clause could be interpreted as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, because this change allows the customer to unfairly benefit. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NYS General Obligations Law § 5-701, addresses agreements required to be in writing, indicating the importance of clear, written contractual obligations. By introducing ambiguity and weakening the non-circumvention clause, the modified text undermines the enforceability of the agreement and contradicts the principle of good faith and fair dealing, which assumes obligations that are clear and agreed upon in writing. This falls under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition, as it makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term and any renewal terms of the Agreement, and for a period of one (1) year following the expiration or earlier termination thereof, Customer shall not, without Kubient's prior written consent, directly or indirectly (i) solicit or encourage any person to leave the employment or other service of Kubient; or (ii) hire, on behalf of Customer or any other person or entity, any person who has left the employment of Kubient within the one (1) year period following the termination or end of that person's employment.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term and any renewal terms of the Agreement, and for a period of one (1) year following the expiration or earlier termination thereof, Customer should, where reasonably practical, avoid soliciting or encouraging any person to leave the employment or other service of Kubient; furthermore, the Customer should also consider not hiring any person who has left the employment of Kubient.
perturbation.explanation: The words 'shall not' are changed to 'should, where reasonably practical, avoid'. Also, instead of 'hire', the change says 'should also consider not hiring', both of which introduce ambiguity and make the obligation non-mandatory. This change weakens the non-solicitation clause, making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_citation: New York Law - Enforcement of Non-Solicitation Agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law generally permits non-solicitation agreements if they are reasonable in time and scope. By weakening the clause's language, the modified text makes it harder for Kubient to prove a breach of contract if the Customer does solicit or hire former employees. The ambiguous language could be interpreted as merely expressing a preference rather than creating a binding legal obligation. The initial agreement was very clear about the restrictions, so the changes are considerable.

(GBS) CHAPTER 20, ARTICLE 26 § 394-c. Limitations on certain contracts involving social referral services. 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "social referral service" shall include any service for a fee providing matching of members, by use of computer or any other means, for the purpose of dating or general social contact. (b) "ancillary services" shall refer to goods or services directly or indirectly related to or to be provided in connection with the social referral service process, including but not limited to photography, grooming, cosmetology, dating etiquette, dating counseling, or other services. (c) "online dating service" shall mean any social referral service where the services are offered primarily online, such as by means of an internet website or a mobile application. perturbation.justification: The original text used 'shall not', creating a clear obligation for the customer. The modified text uses 'should, where reasonably practical, avoid' and 'should also consider not hiring', which introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation non-mandatory, thus weakening the non-solicitation clause. The Law Explanation states that this ambiguous language could be interpreted as merely expressing a preference rather than creating a binding legal obligation. The perturbation weakens the original agreement, which the Law explanation states was initially very clear. This falls under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where the ambiguous obligation introduced makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law. The scraped law snippet 1 references limitations on certain contracts, but the snippet alone doesn't provide specific enough details to support a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree to reconcile revenue numbers and the revenue share calculations no later than 10 days following the end of each calendar month during which the Services were rendered.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will endeavor to reconcile revenue numbers and the revenue share calculations within a reasonable timeframe following the end of each calendar month during which the Services were rendered.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree to reconcile... no later than 10 days' to 'will endeavor to reconcile... within a reasonable timeframe' introduces significant ambiguity. 'Endeavor' implies an effort but not a guarantee of completion. 'Reasonable timeframe' lacks a specific deadline, making enforcement difficult and creating uncertainty around payment schedules.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform
perturbation.law_citation: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UCC primarily governs the sale of goods, its principles regarding reasonable timeframes are often applied to service contracts. By removing the specific 10-day deadline, the modified clause creates uncertainty and potentially allows for unreasonable delays in reconciliation. This could be construed as a failure to perform contractual obligations within a reasonable time, leading to a breach of contract. The original text was very clear on when to reconcile.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a deadline of 'no later than 10 days' for reconciling revenue, while the changed text replaces this with 'within a reasonable timeframe.' This creates an ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of the original agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-309 of the UCC, states that 'the time for... any other action under a contract if not provided... shall be a reasonable time.' While this snippet seems to allow for a 'reasonable time,' it applies when a specific time is *not* provided. Here, the original contract *did* provide a specific time, which was then removed, creating a situation where the 'reasonable time' is significantly less defined and enforceable than the original 10-day deadline, leading to a contradiction of the legal obligation. The change makes the obligation ambiguous and difficult to enforce.
